title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] ALEX VANYO: Hi, I'm Alex,
a developer relations engineer working on
adaptive Android apps. EMILIE ROBERTS: And I am Emilie
I work on Android on ChromeOS. And if you haven't heard
the term "adaptive" before, you are in luck. We are going to take you
through the why, what, and how of adaptive
Android development. We'll talk about why
this is important, about a whole bunch
of new form factors, and how many users are using
your apps on multiple devices. We'll dig a bit deeper into what
exactly the adaptive mindset is. And we'll look at some new tools
in Compose and Android Studio to help you build. We also have an important
Android 16 announcement that could affect your apps in a
big way, so stay tuned for that. So let's start with
where we are now and why it's important, why
it's time to think adaptive. When smartphones
first appeared, they were proof of how far
computing had come. Before the 1960s, computers
could be room-sized boxes full of vacuum tubes
with punch cards. And then you could miraculously
carry massive computing power around in your pocket. And Android apps
unlock the potential of those devices for
billions of users. That's really a paradigm
shift in how many humans interact with the world. ALEX VANYO: As more apps and
games have come to market, users expect them to run well
on all the surfaces they use, from the watch on their wrists
to the Chromebook laptop to the screen in
their parked car, and soon, even
projected into extended reality from their headsets. We think it's important
to design and build apps to meet your users
wherever they are, and to bring your awesome
experiences to them in a way that meets their
expectations and delights them. And adaptive doesn't just
mean basic functionality in all these devices. It also embraces people
with multiple devices in a device ecosystem. These are users who are
invested in your apps, both in terms of
time and their money. So we should offer them
exceptional experiences. Users with more than
one device are often
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw
text:
It also embraces people
with multiple devices in a device ecosystem. These are users who are
invested in your apps, both in terms of
time and their money. So we should offer them
exceptional experiences. Users with more than
one device are often the most engaged users. For example, across six of the
major streaming apps in the US, users that had tablet and phones
had three times the amount of usage than phone-only users. It makes sense to make that
tablet experience cinematic. Since last year, many more apps
have been making optimizations to improve their apps experience
across the device landscape. And your adaptive apps can now
access new device capabilities as well as they extend to more
classes of Android devices. First, on both of our
Android-for-cars platforms, Android Automotive
OS and Android Auto, users stay connected, get
personalized navigation, and can listen to music
during every drive. And starting this year,
adaptive mobile apps in permitted categories can be
distributed on both platforms while parked. This can be the same
mobile app that you distribute to phones,
foldables, and tablets with minimal changes. It's still Android after all. Both platforms provide an
app-compatibility experience that ensures users can
complete the critical journeys within the app. So this combination of
foldables, tablets, Chromebooks, and compatible cars brings us to
over 500 million active devices that your mobile app can run on. And that number will
continue to rise. Second, we're expanding
the number of ways that your app can show up
on large-screen devices. In Android 16, we've made
significant advancements in desktop windowing,
where apps run in fully freeform Windows like
other desktop environments. And when you plug in a phone or
tablet to an external display, that desktop
windowing environment will also appear on the
connected monitor or TV. This means that phones will also
benefit from these improvements, on top of over 500
million active devices.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw
text:
And when you plug in a phone or
tablet to an external display, that desktop
windowing environment will also appear on the
connected monitor or TV. This means that phones will also
benefit from these improvements, on top of over 500
million active devices. In Android 16, we've been adding
foundational platform support for connected displays that
will be available in AOSP in the coming months
in developer preview. And finally, our newest
platform is Android XR. Headsets add a new dimension
to Android, literally. Rather than running on
a traditional screen, apps are placed into a fully
immersive virtual environment, where you have a natural sense
of depth, scale, and realism. Headsets are the first form
factor that Android XR supports. And we'll share more information
about the developer experience for glasses and how you can
participate later this year. Mobile apps form the foundation
of the home space app experience in XR, as mobile apps
distributed through the Play Store will automatically
be available in XR. If you're building for Android,
you're already building for XR. All of the work that you've
made and continue to make in optimizing for different
aspect ratios and arbitrarily resizing directly translates
into a great out-of-the-box experience in XR. And that all leads to
the perfect launching pad to differentiate for XR. You can add an entry point to
launch into full space, where your app runs on its own and
has full control of the content and environment. You can transport users
to a virtual space, load 3D graphics in
your living room, or attach a giant virtual
screen on the wall. So taken all together,
we have a growing number of phones, foldables,
tablets, and Chromebooks. Many of those are capable
of turning into full desktop environments when
plugged into peripherals. And cars and XR will form new
opportunities for running apps. EMILIE ROBERTS: OK,
I'd like to dive more into what we mean
by this adaptive mindset. Adaptive is a strategy
for app development

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw

environments when
plugged into peripherals. And cars and XR will form new
opportunities for running apps. EMILIE ROBERTS: OK,
I'd like to dive more into what we mean
by this adaptive mindset. Adaptive is a strategy
for app development that takes into account the
whole expanding Android device ecosystem and aims to make your
apps run across it, regardless of the screen size
or input method. It has a holistic view of
how your users often own more than one of these devices. And lastly, it considers
new novel devices that haven't even
been created yet and aims to lay down
strong engineering foundations for the future. I do want to reassure
you that you do not have to rebuild your whole
app to take advantage of all these new form factors. And you don't need to build
a brand-new separate app for each one of these and
have six different versions of your app. The mindset that
we're proposing here is that your one app can
run across all of these form factors, so phones, tablets,
foldables, Chromebooks, connected displays,
XR, and auto. Super exciting, right? I see some big smiles. Yeah! [APPLAUSE] And a little bit
daunting, maybe, for some. ALEX VANYO: [LAUGHS] EMILIE ROBERTS: So
throughout the years, I've been working on Chrome OS. Tons of devs, some
of you in this room, actually, have come up to
me and say, hey, Emilie, we just don't have the
time or resources right now to focus on anything other than
that portrait phone experience. And I totally get that. And I hope that
some of the business metrics my good friend Alex
shared with you were compelling. But there's another element here
on the user-facing side that's hard to measure. And I wanted to
hopefully add it in here as an underpainting for you. It's a personal story. So users use your apps
all day long on all their different devices. And at work, at the
office in Paris, we have this amazing music room. And I love to pop
down and play guitar. And I often use a
phone app to play along with the tab or score. But later on, at home, I'm
going to use a tablet or maybe

And at work, at the
office in Paris, we have this amazing music room. And I love to pop
down and play guitar. And I often use a
phone app to play along with the tab or score. But later on, at home, I'm
going to use a tablet or maybe my Chromebook hooked
to a 4K monitor, because there's better
sound and a bigger screen, which is easier for
me to play along with. However, I want that same app
that I know and love on my phone to work on those other devices. I need all my songs, my
progress, my tempo settings. And I need the score
to be the same. If there's any musicians here,
if you're learning a new song, you can't keep switching
between different versions on the same day. At least, I can't. So if the app doesn't work
on one of those form factors that I want to use, I might
look for a better app that does. So the message here
is if your app doesn't work across any of
these form factors, there's a potential you might

And it's built to integrate with
these Compose adaptive layouts. App navigation has always been
a tricky part of app design. And the new updates
make it easier to find user journeys
across all of your screens with less boilerplate. So here's an example
of how it helps. So at smaller window
sizes, like phones, it is likely that
your Lists and Detail panes are separate
destinations, so you're going to display them separately. This makes sense. But when you have enough
window space to show both, you want to show the list
and detail at the same time. But your navigation
flow kind of gets stuck. Our previous recommendation
with Navigation 2 and the adaptive libraries was
to combine your list and detail destinations into a
single, larger destination. And then that one
could show one or both, depending on the window size. This works, but it requires
refactoring to your navigation setup, and it's
not very intuitive. So based on your
feedback, now showing more than one pane at a time
is a foundational principle in the library. What this means
is that you do not have to change your
navigation destination setup if you have separate
lists and detail destinations. Instead, you can modify
the navigation setup to be capable of rendering
multiple destinations at once if and
when space allows. And this also helps drive
predictive back support between destinations. So let's look at a preview of
what that looks like in code. And this drove the
demo you just saw. When setting up the
destinations with Navigation 3, instead of combining
the destinations, we indicate how navigation
should treat our pane through metadata. So is this composable a
List or is it a Detail pane? So here, we have the chats list,
which showed the list of chats, and the chat
thread, which showed the detail for a specific chat. Here, then we add in
the metadata indicating that the chat list should
be considered a list and the chat thread should
be considered a detail. And this allows configuring
how panes are displayed.

the detail for a specific chat. Here, then we add in

we use the Compose adaptive library in the demo. We used an integration
with Navigation 3 that's available
in snapshots today. Just a note, we're still in

these APIs might work for you. And let us know if there
are any tweaks that we can do to make it better
and make it easier for you to build your apps. EMILIE ROBERTS: So in Compose
adaptive library 1.2, which is now in alpha, we've added two
new pane adaptation strategies, levitate and reflow. Let's say I have an app
with an extra info panel that I would show. On a wide screen, I normally

want to surface it to the user, but I don't want to take
up the whole screen. Maybe I even want
to really make it pop to make sure the user's
attention is grabbed by it. So levitate allows
you to take an app and levitate it up to a
vertical level in a dialog window, or also something
like a bottom sheet type implementation. So here's the code for that. We're going to take
that extra pane. And the adaptation strategy
is to levitate it up into a dialog window that
floats on top of other content. I'm only levitating in the
situation where I normally show a single pane only, so a
portrait-type narrower screen. I can also set a scrim
that will slightly blur the underlying
background content. If I'd prefer a bottom
sheet implementation that pulls up over top of
that, I can do that, too. I just set the alignment
and make it draggable. So what if I have the
same kind of situation. I have an extra Info pane,
but on a narrower screen, I maybe don't want
it to float right up

that, I can do that, too. I just set the alignment

reflow strategy. And I'm saying that
extra pane should reflow underneath the Detail pane. [APPLAUSE] Wow. Yes. Awesome. And what about Compose and XR? So there's a cool strategy
here in the Compose adaptive libraries. XR component overrides. You see how the Navigation rail
gets pulled out into an orbiter? The Mail, Chat, Spaces,
and the List-Detail panes get pulled out into
spatialized panes. To enable it, you wrap your
app in Enable XR Component Overrides. And when your app transitions
between 2D and 3D, the overrides do the rest,
built into the Compose adaptive library. So for full details on that and
other Compose approaches in XR, check out this recorded talk. And before moving on,
we've discussed a bunch of powerful coding APIs. But what about the
design side of things? I recognize at least one
designer in the room. I'm sure there's more. For that, how to build
awesome adaptive experiences, check out this recorded talk. ALEX VANYO: So let's
talk a bit more about how Jetpack Compose helps
you to build adaptive apps. Compose is built
in layers, which means that the runtime and
foundational libraries are shared across Android surfaces. That means that improvements
to these libraries around performance, testing,
preview support, tooling, they benefit every form factor. Compose also has strong
input support out of the box. Even if you haven't
tested how your app works with a stylus or mouse,
your users have been. And this usually works
great out of the box. Because components like
clickable and interaction
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw
text:
Compose also has strong
input support out of the box. Even if you haven't
tested how your app works with a stylus or mouse,
your users have been. And this usually works
great out of the box. Because components like
clickable and interaction states, they handle
each of the ways that the users are
interacting with their device. And we're working to
improve this further. On our roadmap for
Compose 1.9, we are adding right-click
context menus in addition to enhanced functionality
for trackpad and mouse. That also means that more
multi-finger gestures are interpreted
automatically out of the box, like triple-tapping
to select a paragraph. Another advantage of Compose is
that all of your layout logic is in a single place
using Kotlin code. When developing different
layouts for different window sizes, you don't have to
maintain parallel layout XML files and keep them in sync with
each other when making changes. Instead, logic can be done at
runtime with normal Kotlin code in composables. Our recommended way to make
top-level layout decisions is to use window
size classes, which are a standardized set
of breakpoints that bucket different window sizes. You can calculate the
current window size class using androidx.window and
the current size of the window with local window info. We define window size
classes individually for both width and height. For height, the window
is an expanded height at 900 DP or more, a medium
height at 480 DP or more, and a compact height
at anything smaller. But because vertical
scrolling is so ubiquitous, width is usually more important. Width works in a similar way,
with breakpoints for an expanded width, 840 DP or higher, medium
width at 600 DP or higher, and compact width
at anything smaller. And for width, we are
introducing two new window size classes, an Androidx
window 1.5, that subdivide the expanded
width-size class bucket further to help adapt layouts to window
sizes common on large tablets and desktop displays. So when using these new
window size classes,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw
text:
classes, an Androidx
window 1.5, that subdivide the expanded
width-size class bucket further to help adapt layouts to window
sizes common on large tablets and desktop displays. So when using these new
window size classes, a extra large width window size
class is at 600 DP or wider, a large is then between
1200 DP up to 1600 DP. And that means that
when you're using these, expanded is now
between 840 and 1200. At a large window size
class width or bigger, there's now enough
horizontal space that the Navigation
rail can expand into a permanent
Navigation drawer. And at an extra large
window size class width, there's enough
horizontal space that you can have three panes
of content side by side, depending on
the content within them. So let's look at how to use
these new window size classes. Here, we're choosing
how many panes to show based on
whether the window width is expanded or larger. To show three panes, an extra
large width window size class, the first step is to update the
window size calculation to use the new set of breakpoints. And this step won't change
an existing behavior because the breakpoints
act as cutoffs. Then you can add a new branch
using that new extra large width window size class breakpoint. So now, when the window
is at least 600 DP wide, we'll show three
panes instead of two. When you're validating
layout logic like this, you can use previews
to get quick feedback across a wide variety of
sizes and aspect ratios. For window-filling
screens, you can also specify different
devices by name to see their respective sizes
and also their inset values. For automated testing,
screenshot tests allow verifying visuals
across different window sizes, both for smaller components
and more complex full-window layouts. And for instrumented
behavior tests, you can use device configuration
override to locally override many configuration values
to simulate behavior on different window
sizes, different font scales, different
layout directions, without having to adjust
device settings from the test

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw

you can use device configuration
override to locally override many configuration values
to simulate behavior on different window
sizes, different font scales, different
layout directions, without having to adjust
device settings from the test or run your tests across a
suite of different emulators. And another device configuration
override you can use is window insets. This allows you to provide
a fake window insets value to help validate
your layouts and behavior for different amounts of insets
as your app goes edge to edge. New Android Studio
tools can also help check how your
app is behaving across each of the environments
that your app is being used in. Journeys with Gemini, available
in Android Studio Narwhal Feature Drop, allows defining
tests using natural language instead of creating
and maintaining tests written using multiple
complex testing frameworks. Here, we use the
natural language to say what we want to do. And the execution taps
on the Plus button and the Accessibility card. And then Journeys with
Gemini interprets the request and makes sure the
output matches. And if it doesn't,
the test fails. Because these journeys work
towards achieving specific goals and validate that flows in
your app can be accomplished, they can be more robust for
different window sizes and form factors. So for example, here, where
we have a multi-pane setup, the exact actions
the user might take might be slightly different
depending on your layout. So instead of having to
encode those differences very specifically in your
tests, and then maybe have different tests for
different window sizes, these higher-level
objectives that you can declare with
natural language are closer to what the user
is actually interested in. Can I find my way to a
specific part of the app and then do something? This new type of testing can
be a great additional tool in your testing
toolbelt to validate your features everywhere
your users are using them. So we've looked at how the
Compose adaptive library can help structure your app flows.

This new type of testing can
be a great additional tool in your testing
toolbelt to validate your features everywhere
your users are using them. So we've looked at how the

app be available? It's one of the easiest ways
to lose potential users. And it often happens if
you declare a feature as required when your app
doesn't actually need it. So any features
like GPS, camera, telephony, that your app
doesn't strictly need, if it's declared in
your Android manifest, it won't show up on the
store on certain devices. So, for example--
oops, I missed a line. Some of these can also
be implicitly required through dependencies on other
features or permissions. So let's say I have
a social media app. And I want to take some photos. And maybe I want
to use GPS to see where a user is to surface
local events in the area. No problem. All I need to do is put
"Android camera" and "GPS" in the manifest. And everything's great, right? AUDIENCE: No. EMILIE ROBERTS: No. That's right. That's not right at all. This will make it only
available on devices with a rear-facing camera or
with an internal GPS chip. But the Chromebook there

a hard requirement, make sure your app

important consideration for these form factors. Tablets that people carry

keyboard and touchpad, or Chromebooks or Detachables

let's talk about resizability. So as we've talked and
seen these new places where apps are running, this is
the most important thread for more general app behavior. For example, in
desktop windowing, both on a built-in display
and on a connected display, apps will be used in a fully
freeform windowing environment. Users can multitask,
move windows around to get something done, or
get distracted while trying to get something done. And users expect those apps to
reflow and reshape accordingly. In XR, apps run in two
different modes, Home space and Full space. And in Home space, a user can
multitask with multiple apps running in 2D window. And you can think
of this like running a bunch of different apps in a
desktop windowing environment
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw
text:
In XR, apps run in two
different modes, Home space and Full space. And in Home space, a user can
multitask with multiple apps running in 2D window. And you can think
of this like running a bunch of different apps in a
desktop windowing environment that has infinite screen space. And that similarity
is no accident. This user expectation that
apps can be resized arbitrarily and support all aspect ratios
is the core reason for one of the biggest
Android 16 changes. The platform will
no longer react to restrictions for orientation,
resizability, and aspect ratios. Before Android 16,
OEMs have already implemented ways for users
to override app aspect ratio and orientation to meet
their expectations. Beginning in Android 16, we're
making this a standard platform behavior on large screens. So starting in Android
16, when targeting SDK 36, restricting orientation,
aspect ratio, and resizability will no
longer always be allowed. On displays that are at least
600 DP in both dimensions, requests for any of the portrait
or landscape orientation values will be ignored. And the activity will be given
the user's preferred orientation anyway. In addition, restrictions to
resizability and aspect ratio will also be ignored. So this means that
activities will be allowed to be
any aspect ratio and will be resizable
in even more situations than they were before. This is a big change. It means that your
apps will need to have layouts that work for
portrait and landscape windows and everything in between,
and support those size changes at runtime. So now, whenever you're
building a new app or screen, build for both
orientations and anticipate resizing from the beginning. Using Compose, you can use
the latest tools and libraries to build an adaptive forward
way from the beginning. And for existing apps,
it is critical to revisit any old assumptions you
might have about window size and what orientations
you need to support. Make sure to bring
it up to date. Some of the most common
issues we've seen, and users have
already seen these
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 15oPNK1W0Tw
text:
any old assumptions you
might have about window size and what orientations
you need to support. Make sure to bring
it up to date. Some of the most common
issues we've seen, and users have
already seen these when overriding apps today, are
layouts where components are cut off, camera viewfinders
that are stretched or skewed in strange ways, and
losing state when resizing or rotation occurs. So when you're
testing your apps, in preparation for
targeting SDK 36, make sure to check all
these different areas, update your layouts, and make
sure you're preserving state correctly. And because this
is a large change, we're doing a few
different things to help achieve a
smooth transition. So first, behavior remains
unchanged for display environments that are not
small [INAUDIBLE] 600 DP. So in particular, this means
that restricting orientation will still work for most
built-in candybar phone displays and the outer
displays of foldables. Second, we are giving a
temporary opt-out manifest flag at both the application
level and the activity level that delays these changes taking
effect until target SDK 37. This allows for more time
to plan for these changes if you need it. And third, users will
continue to have the ability to override app behavior. This both gives
users an escape hatch to disable these
restriction changes and revert back to what the
app is requesting, and also gives the users a way--
continues to give users a way to force all these aspect
ratios and orientations anyway, even if an app hasn't updated to
target SDK 36 yet, or you have and you're using the opt out. And finally, these changes
will not apply to games. Because so many games are
designed for full screen immersive or specific
device orientations, games are currently not opted
into this change by default. This means that if your app
category is Game in the Play Store when using App bundles
and Play-managed app signing, or if you manually specify
the app category to Game in the manifest, you are not
impacted by these changes

category is Game in the Play Store when using App bundles
and Play-managed app signing, or if you manually specify

manually opt in. So in summary, make sure to
test your app on Android 16 thoroughly, both for
these changes as well as the other Android

configuration handling into Unity 6. There's APIs for handling

density changes, which can be a big deal if you

always think about. And you can do that right
from within your game code. We also created a
Unity sample project that can take you step by step
from a completely unoptimized game to add resizing

devices, environments, and platform shifts. These Android 16 changes around
orientation, aspect ratio, and resizability are a result
of the platform changing to meet user expectations. Users expect a great experience
across all their devices, big and small. And mobile Android apps
form the foundation across all of these devices. Devices are becoming more
capable and flexible. Desktop windowing can transform
phones, foldables, and tablets into desktop-like

you can hold in your hand can be used in so

Android XR devices, users will have a whole new

to Compose and tooling. As you update your app
